figures.caption: Table 16: Prompt templates for the Query Reformer (Sec. 3.2 of the main paper). Two submodules serve different purposes: t2v_initialize generates the initial T2V search query at Turn 0 by applying u0 to the reference video, while rewrite_queries compresses the accumulated running query when it exceeds the length threshold at Turn t ≥ 1.

Figure 6: Human-feedback interface. Participants view the reference video, current top-ranked candidate, initial modification text, and an optional system question. They provide free-form feedback in the text box or click “Found It” to terminate the session. The interaction history panel tracks the search progression, and the ground-truth target video is shown at the bottom as the intended search goal.

Table 14: Robustness to degraded simulated feedback on WebVid-CoVR-Test. We report Turn 1 R@1, Turn 5 R@1, the interactive gain from Turn 0 to Turn 5, and BRI at Turn 5. Among the genuinely degraded settings (surface noise, constraint dropping, and modify-only), ReCoVR remains consistently stronger than UMIVR and preserves large multi-turn gains. The rewrite-only setting is included as a strategy-shift control.

Figure 10: Failure case. A large visual gap between the reference (indoor, male) and target (outdoor, female) causes the CoVR channel to remain at rank 168. Despite the T2V channel reaching rank 1, fixed fusion yields a final rank of only 22.

Figure 1: Overview of Interactive Composed Video Retrieval (ICoVR) and ReCoVR. (a) Paradigm Comparison: (1) Traditional CoVR is single-turn only. (2) Naive multi-turn methods use a single channel and an open loop (ignoring past results L̂t0...t−1), causing error compounding. (3) ReCoVR features a dual-pathway architecture: an Intent Pathway routing to CoVR and T2V channels, and a closed-loop Reflection Pathway diagnosing historical trajectories. (b) Interaction Example: Across turns, ReCoVR dynamically extracts latent constraints from user feedback and collaboratively ranks videos to successfully retrieve the target.

Figure 3: ICoVR comparison on additional CoVR benchmarks. Under the same interactive protocol, ReCoVR shows stronger turn-wise improvement than adapted I-TVR baselines on DenseWebVid-CoVR-Test (a,b) and FineCVR-Test (c,d).

Figure 9: Successful case on FineCVR. Fine-grained appearance and action cues are anchored to the visual reference via the CoVR channel, improving the target from rank 7 to rank 1 over two interaction turns.

Table 12: Per-turn latency breakdown of ReCoVR. Time is averaged over all executed interactive turns (N=208). Percentages are computed against the measured end-to-end turn cost in the automatic pipeline. Individual module percentages may not sum exactly to the subtotal due to rounding.

Figure 8: Comparison on the same WebVid-CoVR case. While ReCoVR improves the target from rank 42 to rank 1 in Fig. 7, UMIVR remains stuck at rank 5 under the same target instance and interaction budget, despite receiving explicit scene, airplane-wing, and viewpoint feedback. This comparison highlights the importance of trajectory-level reflection for correcting stalled retrieval.
Table 3: Component Ablation on WebVid-CoVR-Test and FineCVR-Test.

Figure 4: Parameter sensitivity of TW-RRF on WebVid-CoVR-Test (top) and FineCVR-Test (bottom). ReCoVR remains stable across broad ranges of k, top-K cutoff, rank-cap positions, and window size W , suggesting that its gains are not tied to brittle heuristic choices.

Figure 2: Pipeline of ReCoVR. When standard CoVR fails at Turn 0 (a), the system enters an interactive loop. At each turn, the Intent Pathway (b) decomposes user feedback ut into dual-channel queries and generates a clarifying question qt+1, while the Reflection Pathway (d) infers satisfaction σt and constraints ∆ct from the session state, then consults the retrieval trajectory to apply rank penalties ∆rt. Both pathways read from and write to a shared Memory Bank (c) that tracks semantic state (PM) and ranked results (RM). Finally, TW-RRF (e) fuses candidates over a recency-weighted sliding window to produce the final rank list L̂t.

Table 19: Prompt templates for the simulated user (Sec. C.2). The simulator follows a two-stage protocol: Stage 1 classifies the discrepancy between the target and the candidate (MINOR_DIFF and MAJOR_DIFF correspond to the MINOR and MAJOR status in Algorithm 1, respectively); Stage 2 selects one of three feedback policies accordingly. All methods in our experiments share this identical simulator.

Figure 7: Successful case on WebVid-CoVR. The user progressively specifies scene details across two turns, improving the target from rank 42 to rank 1. The T2V channel drives convergence after the user provides a holistic scene description in Turn 2.

Table 15: Prompt template for the Intent Decomposer (Sec. 3.2 of the main paper). Given user feedback ut, the module decomposes it into a target-descriptive search query and an optional edit instruction, corresponding to the T2V and CoVR channels respectively.
Table 4: Generalization to composed image retrieval on FashionIQ. ReCoVR consistently outperforms single-turn or interactive methods across all categories.
Table 18: Prompt template for Question Asking (Sec. 3.2 of the main paper). The module identifies remaining ambiguities from the current session state and generates one clarifying question per turn.

Table 17: Prompt template for the State Reflector (Sec. 3.2 of the main paper). The reflection_context variable is populated from PM S t−1 (accumulated queries, prior positive/negative constraints) and relevant gallery captions from PML, corresponding to the inputs

Table 7: Effect of transplanting Rank-cap into open-loop interactive baselines on WebVid-CoVRTest. Turn 0 is omitted because all methods share the same initialization. While Rank-cap consistently improves both IVR and UMIVR, ReCoVR remains the best across all turns, indicating that rank-space intervention alone does not account for the full gains.
